question_text: Arrange the following sets of compounds in order of their increasing boiling points: Pentan-1-ol, butan-1-ol, butan-2-ol, ethanol, propan-1-ol, methanol. Pentan-1-ol, n-butane, pentanal, ethoxy ethane.

Arrange the following sets of compounds in order of their increasing boiling points:

  1. Pentan-1-ol, butan-1-ol, butan-2-ol, ethanol, propan-1-ol, methanol.
  2. Pentan-1-ol, n-butane, pentanal, ethoxy ethane.
solution_text: The order of increasing boiling points is as follows: Methanol, ethanol, propan-1-ol, butan-2-ol, butan-1-ol, pentan-1-ol. n-Butane, ethoxyethane, pentanal, nd pentane-1-ol.
solution_html:
The order of increasing boiling points is as follows:

  1. Methanol, ethanol, propan-1-ol, butan-2-ol, butan-1-ol, pentan-1-ol.
  2. n-Butane, ethoxyethane, pentanal, nd pentane-1-ol.

question_text: The following is not an appropriate reaction for the preparation of t-butylethyl ether. {tex}{C_2}{H_5} - ONa + C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Cl \to C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - O{C_2}{H_5}{/tex} What would be the major product of this reaction? Write a suitable reaction for the preparation of t-butylethyl ether.
question_html:
The following is not an appropriate reaction for the preparation of t-butylethyl ether.
{tex}{C_2}{H_5} - ONa + C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Cl \to C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - O{C_2}{H_5}{/tex}

  1. What would be the major product of this reaction?
  2. Write a suitable reaction for the preparation of t-butylethyl ether.
solution_text: The major product of the given reaction is 2-methylprop-l-ene. It is because sodium ethoxide is a strong nucleophile as well as a strong base. Thus, elimination reaction predominates over substitution. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - {}^ - \underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} N{a^ + }}\limits_{Sodium\;tert.\;butoxide}^{} + C{H_3}C{H_2}Cl \to \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - O{C_2}{H_5}}\limits_{2 - methyl - 2 - methoxypropane} }\limits_{(tert - butylethyl\;ether)} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. The major product of the given reaction is 2-methylprop-l-ene. It is because sodium ethoxide is a strong nucleophile as well as a strong base. Thus, elimination reaction predominates over substitution.
  2. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - {}^ - \underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} N{a^ + }}\limits_{Sodium\;tert.\;butoxide}^{} + C{H_3}C{H_2}Cl \to \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - O{C_2}{H_5}}\limits_{2 - methyl - 2 - methoxypropane} }\limits_{(tert - butylethyl\;ether)} {/tex}

question_text: Give structures of the products you would expect when each of the following alcohol reacts with (a) HCl -ZnCl 2 HBr and SOCl 2 . Butan-1-ol 2-Methylbutan-2-ol
question_html:
Give structures of the products you would expect when each of the following alcohol reacts with (a) HCl -ZnCl2 HBr and SOCl2.

  1. Butan-1-ol
  2. 2-Methylbutan-2-ol
solution_text: When alcohol reacts with HCl-ZnCl 2 the product are {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2}\mathop { - C{H_2} - }\limits_{{\text{Butan - 1 - ol}}} C{H_2} - OH\xrightarrow{{HCl - ZnC{l_2}}}{/tex} No reaction Primary alcohols do not react appreciably with Lucas' reagent (HCl- {tex}ZnCl_2{/tex} ) at room temperature. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{OH} - C{H_3}}\limits_{{\text{2 - Methylbutan - 2 - ol}}(3^\circ )} \xrightarrow{{HCl - ZnC{l_2}}}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{Cl} - C{H_3} + {H_2}O}\limits_{{\text{2 - Chloro - 2 - Methylbutane}}} }\limits_{(White\;turbidity)} {/tex} Tertiary alcohols react immediately with Lucas' reagent. when alcohol react with HBr {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{{\text{Butan - 1 - ol}}} + {\text{HBr}}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}Br}\limits_{{\text{1 - Bromobutane}}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{OH} - C{H_3} + HBr}\limits_{{\text{2 - Methylbutan - 2 - ol}}(3^\circ )} \to \mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{Br} - C{H_3} + {H_2}O}\limits_{{\text{2 - Bromo - 2 - Methlbutane}}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{{\text{Butan - l - ol}}} + SOC{l_2} \to \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}Cl}\limits_{{\text{l - chlorobutane}}} + S{O_2} + HCl{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{OH} - C{H_3} + SOC{l_2}}\limits_{{\text{2 - Methylbutan - 2 - ol}}} \to \mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{Cl} - C{H_3} + S{O_2} + HCl}\limits_{{\text{2 - Chloro - 2Methylbutane}}} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. When alcohol reacts with HCl-ZnCl2 the product are
    1. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2}\mathop { - C{H_2} - }\limits_{{\text{Butan - 1 - ol}}} C{H_2} - OH\xrightarrow{{HCl - ZnC{l_2}}}{/tex} No reaction
      Primary alcohols do not react appreciably with Lucas' reagent (HCl-{tex}ZnCl_2{/tex}) at room temperature.
    2. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{OH} - C{H_3}}\limits_{{\text{2 - Methylbutan - 2 - ol}}(3^\circ )} \xrightarrow{{HCl - ZnC{l_2}}}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{Cl} - C{H_3} + {H_2}O}\limits_{{\text{2 - Chloro - 2 - Methylbutane}}} }\limits_{(White\;turbidity)} {/tex}
      Tertiary alcohols react immediately with Lucas' reagent.
  2. when alcohol react with HBr
    1. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{{\text{Butan - 1 - ol}}} + {\text{HBr}}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}Br}\limits_{{\text{1 - Bromobutane}}} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Alcohols are comparatively more soluble in water than hydrocarbons of comparable molecular masses. Explain this fact.
question_html: Alcohols are comparatively more soluble in water than hydrocarbons of comparable molecular masses. Explain this fact.
solution_text: Alcohols form H-bonds with water due to the presence of -OH group. However, hydrocarbons cannot form H-bonds with water. As a result, alcohols are comparatively more soluble in water than hydrocarbons of comparable molecular masses.
solution_html:
Alcohols form H-bonds with water due to the presence of -OH group. However, hydrocarbons cannot form H-bonds with water.

As a result, alcohols are comparatively more soluble in water than hydrocarbons of comparable molecular masses.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is meant by hydroboration-oxidation reaction? Illustrate it with an example.
question_html: What is meant by hydroboration-oxidation reaction? Illustrate it with an example.
solution_text: The addition of borane followed by oxidation is known as the hydroboration-oxidation reaction. For example, propan-1-ol is produced by the hydroboration-oxidation reaction of propene. In this reaction, propene reacts with diborane {tex}{\left( {B{H_3}} \right)^2}{/tex} to form trialkyl borane as an addition product. This addition product is oxidized to alcohol by hydrogen peroxide in the presence of aqueous sodium hydroxide.
solution_html:
The addition of borane followed by oxidation is known as the hydroboration-oxidation reaction.

For example, propan-1-ol is produced by the hydroboration-oxidation reaction of propene. In this reaction, propene reacts with diborane {tex}{\left( {B{H_3}} \right)^2}{/tex} to form trialkyl borane as an addition product. This addition product is oxidized to alcohol by hydrogen peroxide in the presence of aqueous sodium hydroxide.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain why is ortho nitrophenol more acidic than ortho methoxyphenol?
question_html: Explain why is ortho nitrophenol more acidic than ortho methoxyphenol?
solution_text: The nitro-group is an electron-withdrawing group. The presence of this group in the ortho position decreases the electron density in the O-H bond. As a result, it is easier to lose a proton. Also, the o-nitrophenoxide ion formed after the loss of protons is stabilized by resonance. Hence, ortho nitrophenol is a stronger acid. On the other hand, methoxy group is an electron-releasing group. Thus, it increases the electron density in the O-H bond and hence, the proton cannot be given out easily. For this reason, ortho-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-methoxyphenol.

On the other hand, methoxy group is an electron-releasing group. Thus, it increases the electron density in the O-H bond and hence, the proton cannot be given out easily. For this reason, ortho-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-methoxyphenol.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain the following with an example. Kolbe's reaction. Reimer-Tiemann reaction. Williamson ether synthesis. Unsymmetrical ether.
question_html:
Explain the following with an example.

  1. Kolbe's reaction.
  2. Reimer-Tiemann reaction.
  3. Williamson ether synthesis.
  4. Unsymmetrical ether.
solution_text: Kolbe's reaction: When phenol is treated with sodium hydroxide, sodium phenoxide is produced. This sodium phenoxide when treated with carbon dioxide, followed by acidification, undergoes electrophilic substitution to give ortho-hydroxybenzoic acid as the main product. This reaction is known as Kolbe's reaction. Reimer-Tiemann reaction: When phenol is treated with chloroform (CHCl 3 ) in the presence of sodium hydroxide, a -CHO group is introduced at the ortho position of the benzene ring. This reaction is known as the Reimer-Tiemann reaction. The intermediate is hydrolyzed in the presence of alkalis to produce salicyclaldehyde. Williamson ether synthesis: Williamson ether synthesis is a laboratory method to prepare symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers by allowing alkyl halides to react with sodium alkoxides. This reaction involves attack of the alkoxide ion on the alkyl halide. Better results are obtained in case of primary alkyl halides. If the alkyl halide is secondary or tertiary, then elimination competes over substitution. Unsymmetrical ether: An unsymmetrical ether is an ether where two groups on the two sides of an oxygen atom differ (i.e., have an unequal number of carbon atoms). For example: ethyl methyl ether (CH 3 - O - CH 2 CH 3 )
solution_html:
1. Kolbe's reaction:
    When phenol is treated with sodium hydroxide, sodium phenoxide is produced. This sodium phenoxide when treated with carbon dioxide, followed by acidification, undergoes electrophilic substitution to give ortho-hydroxybenzoic acid as the main product. This reaction is known as Kolbe's reaction.
  2. Reimer-Tiemann reaction: When phenol is treated with chloroform (CHCl3) in the presence of sodium hydroxide, a -CHO group is introduced at the ortho position of the benzene ring.

    This reaction is known as the Reimer-Tiemann reaction. The intermediate is hydrolyzed in the presence of alkalis to produce salicyclaldehyde.
  3. Williamson ether synthesis: Williamson ether synthesis is a laboratory method to prepare symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers by allowing alkyl halides to react with sodium alkoxides.

    This reaction involves attack of the alkoxide ion on the alkyl halide. Better results are obtained in case of primary alkyl halides.

    If the alkyl halide is secondary or tertiary, then elimination competes over substitution.
  4. Unsymmetrical ether: An unsymmetrical ether is an ether where two groups on the two sides of an oxygen atom differ (i.e., have an unequal number of carbon atoms). For example: ethyl methyl ether (CH3 - O - CH2CH3)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Give reason for the higher boiling point of ethanol in comparison to methoxymethane.
question_html: Give reason for the higher boiling point of ethanol in comparison to methoxymethane.
solution_text: Ethanol undergoes intermolecular H-bonding due to the presence of -OH group, resulting in the association of molecules. Extra energy is required to break these hydrogen bonds. On the other hand, methoxymethane does not undergo H-bonding. Hence, the boiling point of ethanol is higher than that of methoxymethane.

solution_text: The reaction of Williamson synthesis involves {tex}S_N^2{/tex} attack of an alkoxide ion on a primary alkyl halide. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - \mathop {\underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} }\limits^ - \mathop {Na}\limits^ + + C{H_3} - Cl{/tex} {tex} \to C{H_3} - \underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - C{H_3} + NaCl{/tex} But if secondary or tertiary alkyl halides are taken in place of primary alkyl halides, then elimination would compete over substitution. As a result, alkenes would be produced. This is because alkoxides are nucleophiles as well as strong bases. Hence, they react with alkyl halides, which results in an elimination reaction. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {{\text{ }}C}\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Cl + Na\mathop O\limits_{ \bullet \bullet }^{\overline { \bullet \bullet } } - C{H_3}}\limits_{Tertiary\,\,alkyl\,halide} \to \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } = + C{H_3}OH + NaCl}\limits_{Alkene} {/tex}

But if secondary or tertiary alkyl halides are taken in place of primary alkyl halides, then elimination would compete over substitution. As a result, alkenes would be produced. question_text: Preparation of ethers by acid dehydration of secondary or tertiary alcohols is not a suitable method. Give reason.
question_html: Preparation of ethers by acid dehydration of secondary or tertiary alcohols is not a suitable method. Give reason.
solution_text: The formation of ethers by dehydration of alcohol is a bimolecular reaction ( {tex}S_N^2{/tex} ) involving the attack of an alcohol molecule on a protonated alcohol molecule. In the method, the alkyl group should be unhindered. In case of secondary or tertiary alcohols, the alkyl group is hindered. As a result, elimination dominates substitution. Hence, in place of ethers, alkenes are formed.

question_text: Explain the fact that in aryl alkyl ethers The alkoxy group activates the benzene ring towards electrophilic substitution and It directs the incoming substituents to ortho and para positions in benzene ring.
question_html:
Explain the fact that in aryl alkyl ethers

  1. The alkoxy group activates the benzene ring towards electrophilic substitution and
  2. It directs the incoming substituents to ortho and para positions in benzene ring.
solution_text: In aryl alkyl ethers, due to the +R effect of the alkoxy group, the electron density in the benzene ring increases as shown in the following resonance structure. Thus, benzene is activated towards electrophilic substitution by the alkoxy group. It can also be observed from the resonance structures that the electron density increases more at the ortho and para positions than at the meta position. As a result, the incoming substituents are directed to the ortho and para positions in the benzene ring.

1. In aryl alkyl ethers, due to the +R effect of the alkoxy group, the electron density in the benzene ring increases as shown in the following resonance structure.

  2. It can also be observed from the resonance structures that the electron density increases more at the ortho and para positions than at the meta position. As a result, the incoming substituents are directed to the ortho and para positions in the benzene ring.

question_text: Show how would you synthesise the following alcohols from appropriate alkenes?
question_html: Show how would you synthesise the following alcohols from appropriate alkenes?
solution_text: The given alcohols can be synthesized by applying Markovnikov's rule of acid-catalyzed hydration of appropriate alkenes. Acid-catalyzed hydration of pent-2-ene also produces pentan-2-ol but along with pentan-3-ol. Thus, the first reaction is preferred over the second one to get pentan-2-ol.

question_text: When 3-methylbutan-2-ol is treated with HBr, the following reaction takes place: {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{C{H_3}} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{HBr}}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{Br} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}{/tex} Give a mechanism for this reaction.
question_html:
When 3-methylbutan-2-ol is treated with HBr, the following reaction takes place:
{tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{C{H_3}} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{HBr}}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{Br} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}{/tex}
Give a mechanism for this reaction.
solution_text: In this reaction, the secondary carbocation formed in step II rearranges to a more stable tertiary carbocation by a hydride ion shift from the 3rd carbon atom.
